titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Woodberry Family Papers
titlestmt.author: Abigail Kromminga

controlnote.p: This collection contains 3 published digital objects. And those objects are comprised of 112 digital files.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday December 5, 2025 at 18:02

did.abstract: This collection contains correspondence, diaries, and collected materials from Earle and Ada Woodberry's missionary work in China (1930-1945), Korea (1950-1954), and Taiwan (1954-1962). Limited correspondence and diaries date from Earle's work with the OSS during WWII. Substantial materials date from Earle's work in Prisoner of War Camps during the Korean War, ministering to Chinese Communist prisoners. The collection provides insight into a half century of war in East Asia, the movements of American empire, and the role of Christianity in the forming Cold War.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Woodberry Family Papers (RG 343), Special Collections, Yale Divinity Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Earle John Woodberry was born in 1892 in Michigan. The child of Methodist missionaries John and Kitty Woodberry, Earle grew up in Shanghai and Tianjin (Tientsin), evacuating amidst the Boxer Rebellion of 1900. In the United States, he attended the Mount Hermon School, the University of New York, the Nyack Missionary Training Institute, and Gordon College. He began his lifelong career as a missionary teacher, minister, and administrator in Shanghai in 1915. In 1918, he was sent as a YMCA secretary to work with Czech and Chinese soldiers trapped in Siberia by the end of WWI and the Russian Revolution. In 1922, on furlough in the United States, he married Ada Pierce, a graduate of Hunter College and Gordon College, who worked as a music teacher in Worcester and played piano at revivals across New England.

In 1924, Earle and Ada moved to Shanghai with the Christian and Missionary Alliance, relocating north to Shandong province (in Penglai and Linyi) with the Presbyterian Board of Foreign Missions after the Shanghai massacre of 1927. At outbreak of the Sino-Japanese War in 1937, Earle, Ada, and their five children sought refuge in the United States, returning quickly to China only to be interned by the Japanese occupation in 1941, held in house arrest and separated from their children at the China Inland Mission School in Yantai (Chefoo).

In 1942, the Woodberrys were among the first prisoners exchanged between the United States and Japan, repatriated on the Italian Conte Verde before transferring to the Swedish Gripsholm. Upon his return to the United States, the Office of Strategic Services (OSS) reached out to Earle for intelligence information. By early 1944 he was back in China working for the OSS on a mission Ada later described as "interesting though dangerous."

In 1949, with China closed to missionary work, Earle and Ada moved to Daegu (Taegu), Korea. Six months later, they were evacuated to Japan as North Korean troops aided by Communist China pushed down the Korean peninsula into territory under American military occupation. During the Korean War, while Ada remained as a teacher in Japan, Earle worked as a United States military chaplain first assisting refugees from the North and then evangelizing captured Chinese Communist soldiers in United Nations Command POW camps in Goeje and on Jeju Island. In the ideological battlefield of the Cold War, Earle's evangelistic work played a vital role. In the 1955 citation for the medal of honor, Earle was credited with converting thousands of Chinese soldiers who elected to be repatriated to Taiwan (Formosa) rather than mainland China. At the end of the war, with Korea indefinitely severed in two, the American army chaplain's office celebrated the defecting soldiers as "the Communists' greatest defeat, their worst embarrassment."

Upon the invitation of Madame Chiang Kai-shek and the Women's Prayer Group, Earle and Ada moved to Taiwan in 1954. During this time, Earle worked in hospital chaplaincy and Ada in Christian education. They continued to advocate for the integration and care of repatriated soldiers from the Korean War.
The Woodberrys retired and moved to the United States in 1962. Earle John Woodberry died in 1981 and Ada Pierce Woodberry in 1995.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Earle and Ada Woodberry's tenure as missionaries in East Asia saw a half century of unrest and war, including the Second Sino-Japanese War, the Chinese Civil War, the American occupation of Korea, the Korean War, and the establishment of the Republic of China on Taiwan. This collection contains materials (correspondence, diaries, and collected pamphlets) from their work, including Earle's time in the OSS in China during WWII and in POW camps in Korea during the Korean War. Further materials from Ada provide insight into her devotional and spiritual life. The collection illustrates the changing political landscape of East Asia in the twentieth century and the integration of Christianity and American anti-Communism in the nascent Cold War.

Series I, Correspondence, 1918 to 1990, is divided into two subseries: circular letters sent out by the Woodberrys describing their work to supporters, and personal correspondence, containing letters from friends and family. Substantial correspondence between Ada and Earle during their years of separation throughout the Korean War (1950-1953) contains Earle's descriptions of United Nations military operations and POW camps. Further descriptions can be found in correspondence from this time.

Series II, Diaries and notebooks, 1906-1990, is organized into two subseries containing materials from Earle and Ada. Notably, Earle's notebooks from 1944-1946 provide a near-daily record of his activities in China while stationed with the OSS. Further notebooks contain sermon notes from POW camps and a record of meetings in Taiwan with individuals including John Foster Dulles and Chiang Kai-shek.

Series III, Topical files, is first organized chronologically according to Earle and Ada's missionary appointments: China (1924-1946), Korea (1950-1954), and Taiwan (1954-1965). Materials relating to the Woodberry family's 1942 repatriation and Earle's military service in China can be found in the subseries "WWII and the Japanese occupation." In the Korea files, "Military chaplaincy" contains Earle's official reports on camp activity, American military documentation, and Earle and Ada's narratives of his work. Letters from former POWs can be found in the Taiwan files, as well as correspondence from Madame Chiang Kai-shek and her prayer group.
Further subseries in Series III contain miscellaneous collected materials—such as newspaper clippings—and concise collections relating to other individuals.

Series IV, Devotional writings and materials, 1888-1990, contains materials from Ada Pierce Woodberry's devotional life and teachings. The series reflects a devotional life that encounters God through scriptural and imagistic reflection—which she sought to share in her monograph Scripturama in Story and Picture and educational curriculum. The "Folders organized by subject" often retain Ada Pierce Woodberry's original order, holding materials spanning decades, article clippings, sketches, notecards, and charts. "Collected materials" contains Christian publications by others.

Series V, Biographical documentation, 1909-1993, includes Earle and Ada's resumes, CVs, and ephemera from their career and retirement. Notably, the subseries "Woodberry family" includes reports from John and Kitty Woodberry, Earle's parents, on their work in China in the first decade of the twentieth century.

Series VI, Photographs, 1910-1993, begins with photographs from Earle and Ada's missionary work, organized by country. Notable within these files are images from Earle's military work in China during WWII, large prints depicting his chaplaincy work in POW camps in the Korean War, pictures from a Korean orphanage including with the American Baptist minister Bob Pierce, and images of POWs in Taiwan. The "Family photographs" include portraits and pictures with family and friends.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes from Beidaihe District (Peitaiho) and Changli.

did.unittitle: Addressed from Korea, Japan, and Taiwan
Addressed from Taiwan and the United States

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes October 2, 1942 letter from a fellow passenger on the Gripsholm recounting their time on the ship.

scopecontent.p: Addressed from the China Inland Mission School in Yantai (Chefoo).

scopecontent.p: Addressed from the China Inland Mission School in Yantai (Chefoo).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes substantial correspondence addressed from EJW while in China with the Office of Strategic Services (OSS).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed from the Stony Brook School.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folders dating from 1950-1953 contain extensive correspondence from EJW describing his work in the prisoner of war (POW) camps in Korea with the United States Military.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dating from EJW's activities in China during WWII.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dating from EJW's time in Daegu (Taegu), Korea and the ensuing evacuation to Japan.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dating from EJW's work in Korea in POW camps.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes notes for sermons in POW camps and Chinese language practice.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dating from EJW's work in Korea and Taiwan.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains notes on deaths and funerals during the Korean War and sermon notes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The following folders from EJW all date from Taiwan.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes notes on EJW's work in POW camps.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dating from EJW and APW's trip to the Anti-Communist Conference in Taiwan.

EJW notes of Chinese politics and culture
APW notes and essays from North China Union Language School

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "City Church Report," 1940, and "Ichowfu Station Report," 1941-1942.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Essay by EJW (?) descibing evangelism techniques.

did.unittitle: Circulars and pamphlets from China missionaries

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes the pamphlets "In China Today…" c. 1938, "Friendship Frontier China" n.d., and a circular from Herbert and Lottie Alexander on their time in Tenghsien during WWII, 1946.

did.unittitle: Map "Field and Work of the Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A. Ichowfu, Shantung"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes their experiences of repatriation and EJW's return to China with the American military.

did.unittitle: Correspondence and receipts regarding liquidation of assets in China
Application for repatriation and evacuation instructions
Correspondence and reports regarding Gripsholm repatriation

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence from the OSS soliciting information and EJW's report "Tengchow."

EJW writings "Personal Report Liason Officer" and "The Call of China"
Correspondence regarding U.S. Military work in China
Correspondence regarding payment from the Presbyterian Board and OSS

descriptivenote.p: Correspondence regarding payment from the Presbyterian Board and OSS

did.unittitle: Chinese language material regarding the reconstruction of Nanjing
"Shantung and its Foreign Rulers," The Current History Magazine

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "China as a Teacher of Christianity" and "Civil War as a Profession in China."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes articles on General Marshal Feng Yu-hsiang and his writing "What China Needs Today."

did.unittitle: Chinese language gospel weekly from Shandong and "Handmaid of the Lord" from the Tientsin Woman's Club

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes cartoons, the song to the tune of Old MacDonald "In South Shantung We Had a War" n.d., and materials about Weixian ("Weihsian") concentration camp.

did.unittitle: Plays about missionaries in China "Decision at Dusk" and "The Reunion"
Afraid? Of What? And Other Poems and Sketches by E.H. Hamilton and miscellaneous booklets

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writings and reminiscenses from a missionary also in Shandong.

did.unittitle: Presbyterian Board of Foreign Missions work

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence on the evacuation of missionaries from Korea.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains a speech welcoming the Woodberrys to Korea by S.H. Kim and "On Cheerfulness" by Su In Je on the U.S. military occupation of Korea.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a translation of a letter from thirty four POWs to General Matthew B. Ridgeway.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "Provisions Regarding the Attendance of News Media Representatives in the Demilitarized Zone," September 16, 1953.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a January 7 letter regarding the interrogation of a sixteen-year-old POW, a February 1 press release announcing EJW's departure, and the October 23 general order "Promulgating the Regulations Governing Religious Activities in the Armed Forces."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "The General Speaks," a tract from Major General W.K. Harrison on the benefits of Christianity.

did.unittitle: EJW "Where the Supply of Scriptures is Never Enough," Bible Society Record
EJW "Miracles of Grace in Chinese POW Camps," The Evangelical Christian
EJW and APW "Six Months in the Chinese Prisoner of War Camp"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For correspondence from EJW describing his work, from which these writings draw, see also: Correspondence, Series I. Box 1, folder 16 to box 2, folder 10.

did.unittitle: EJW "Release to civillian life of Wang Hsiu Ch'ing"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On the capture, conversion, and work of Wang Hsiu Ch'ing.

did.unittitle: EJW "A Vessel for the Master's Use" and other addresses

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Materials potentially prepared for EJW's acceptance of the Medal of Freedom. See also: Medal of Freedom ceremony, box 13, folder 3.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence with Archie R. Crouch.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

"Letters to Tall Soldiers from Small Koreans"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Papers from Earle's sisters, Ethel and Ora, who worked as missionary teachers in Shanghai.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes Ethel and Ora's id cards from their time in the prison in Manila and documentation related to repatriation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Stephen Ma first encountered the Woodberrys in Taiwan, and converted from Islam to Christianity. With their support he attended medical school in Turkey, later working as a physician in the United Kingdom.

did.unittitle: Writings on Stepehn and regarding his baptism

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "Haigazian College: After Ten Years."

did.unittitle: "It Isn't So Hard As We Think!"
Article published in Outreach , stewardship chart, drafts

title.part: Psalms and Hymns and Spiritual Songs: English - Chinese Edition

did.unittitle: Scripturama in Korea and second Asian edition

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writing dedicated to the children on the Gripsholm.

"Gospel in Easy English" project in English and Chinese
Gospel and symbol draft in Chinese

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Done with help from Stephen Ma.

did.unittitle: Gospel and symbol draft in Chinese

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These folders contain a mixture of notes, clippings, and collected materials potentially used for curriculum, dating from the 1930s-1980s. Folders titled with quotation marks retain APW's labeling and original order.

did.unittitle: "From thence He shall come to judge the quick and the dead"
The Study of Words by Richard Chenevix Trench
Union and Communion by J. Hudson Taylor, Facing Calvary by William Evans

The Philosophy of the Cross booklets

scopecontent.p: Includes images of religious services in the camp.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a picture with Pastor Byung Hyuk Han who worked with Earle in the camps.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs of Korean children and life in the orphanages. Includes images of Bob Pierce visiting and writings on Korean orphans.

scopecontent.p: From APW's work in Christian education in Japan while EJW worked in Korea.
